title: Introduction to HTML5 & Basic Structure
description: Learn what HTML5 is, how the web works, and create your first webpage from scratch. This lesson is designed for absolute beginners with no prior coding experience. By the end, you will have built a real HTML file and understand the core structure of any website.
learningObjectives: Explain what HTML5 is and why it's important
Describe the basic steps of how a webpage loads in your browser
Identify and use the basic structure tags: !DOCTYPE, html, head, title, body
Add headings, paragraphs, line breaks, and formatted text to a webpage
Write comments in HTML to explain your code
Create and open your first .html file in a browser

subtopics.title: What is HTML5? (For Complete Beginners)
subtopics.content:
HTML, or HyperText Markup Language, is the foundational language used to create and structure web pages on the internet. It serves as the skeleton of every website, defining the content and organization of elements such as headings, paragraphs, images, links, and other media, so that web browsers can interpret and display them correctly. In essence, HTML is a **markup language** — not a programming language — that uses special tags to label and describe different parts of a document. These tags tell the browser what each piece of content is (for example, a heading, a paragraph, or an image), enabling the seamless presentation of information across the World Wide Web.
The history of HTML dates back to the early 1990s. It was invented by British computer scientist **Tim Berners-Lee** while he was working at CERN, the European Organization for Nuclear Research in Switzerland. In 1989, Berners-Lee proposed a system to allow researchers to easily share and link documents across different computers. This idea led to the creation of the World Wide Web. In 1991, he released the first version, known as HTML 1.0, which was a simple markup language designed primarily for sharing scientific documents with basic text formatting and hyperlinks.
Over the following years, HTML evolved rapidly. By 1995, HTML 2.0 became the first standardized version. HTML 3.2 and HTML 4.01 followed, introducing more features like tables, forms, and better support for multimedia. During this period, browser wars between companies like Netscape and Microsoft led to many unofficial extensions, causing compatibility issues across different browsers.
In the early 2000s, a stricter version called XHTML was introduced to bring more consistency and XML-like rules to HTML. However, it proved too rigid for many developers. The major breakthrough came with **HTML5**, which was officially standardized in 2014 after years of development by the World Wide Web Consortium (W3C) and the Web Hypertext Application Technology Working Group (WHATWG). HTML5 brought significant improvements, including native support for video and audio, semantic elements (like header, nav, article, and footer), better form controls, canvas for graphics, and enhanced mobile and accessibility features.
Today, HTML5 is the universally accepted standard, powering nearly all modern websites and applications. It continues to evolve with new capabilities added over time, remaining the essential starting point for anyone building anything on the web.
In brief, HTML transformed the way information is shared globally, turning the internet from a network of isolated documents into an interconnected, accessible world of rich, structured content. Without it, the modern web as we know it would not exist.
commonMisconceptions: HTML is not a programming language (it's a markup language – it doesn't perform logic or calculations).
You don't need special paid software – any basic text editor works.
subtopics.realWorldAnalogy: HTML is like the blueprint of a house. The blueprint tells the builder where the walls, doors, and windows go, just like HTML tells the browser where the headings, paragraphs, and images go.

subtopics.title: Structure of an HTML Document
subtopics.content:
HTML Structure refers to the hierarchical, tree-like arrangement of elements in an HTML document, consisting of nested tags that define the document's content, metadata, and layout, typically beginning with !DOCTYPE html tag and containing html, head, and body tag sections. Every HTML document follows a standard structure that the browser expects. Understanding this structure is the first step to writing correct HTML. Let's look at each part in detail. The **DOCTYPE declaration** is the document type declaration that tells the web browser which version of HTML the page is written in. For modern web development, it declares the document as HTML5 and must be the very first line of any HTML file. The **DOCTYPE declaration** is written as shown below: ```html ``` This line tells the browser: "This document is written in HTML5." Without it, browsers may render your page in 'quirks mode', which can cause unexpected display issues. Next comes the **html element**, which wraps the entire document: ```html ... ``` The `lang` attribute tells search engines and screen readers what language your page is written in. Inside the html element, you have two main sections — **head** and **body**. The **head** section contains information *about* the page that is not directly visible to users: ```html My First Webpage ``` - `meta charset` sets the character encoding (UTF-8 supports almost all characters worldwide). - `meta viewport` ensures your page scales correctly on mobile devices. - `title` sets the text shown in the browser tab. The **body** section contains everything visible on the page: ```html

Hello, World!

Welcome to my first webpage.

``` Putting it all together, here is the minimal boilerplate for any HTML5 page: ```html My Page

Hello, World!

This is my first HTML page.

``` Every webpage you will ever build starts with this exact skeleton. Memorise it — it is the foundation of everything.
commonMisconceptions: The head tag is NOT where you put visible content — it is for metadata only.
Forgetting DOCTYPE does not crash the page, but it can cause subtle layout bugs in older browsers.
The title tag is NOT the same as an h1 heading — it only appears in the browser tab, not on the page.
subtopics.realWorldAnalogy: Think of an HTML document like a letter. The head is the envelope — it carries information about the letter (sender, recipient, date) but you never read it out loud. The body is the actual letter content that everyone reads.

subtopics.title: Headings, Paragraphs & Text Formatting
subtopics.content:
Now that you understand the structure, let's add real content to the body. HTML gives you several tags for organising and formatting text. **Headings** range from h1 (largest/most important) to h6 (smallest/least important): ```html

Main Page Title

Section Title

Sub-section Title

Smaller Heading

Even Smaller
Smallest Heading
``` Search engines use headings to understand the hierarchy of your content. Always use one h1 per page. **Paragraphs** are created with the p tag: ```html

This is a paragraph of text. Browsers automatically add space above and below paragraphs.

This is a second paragraph. Notice the gap between them.

``` **Line breaks** insert a single line break without creating a new paragraph: ```html

Line one.
Line two is on the next line but in the same paragraph.

``` **Text formatting** tags give meaning or emphasis to words: ```html

This word is bold and important.

This word is italicised for emphasis.

This text is highlighted.

This text is struck through.

Water is H2O — subscript example.

E=mc2 — superscript example.

``` Here is a complete example bringing it all together: ```html Text Formatting

Welcome to CodeBay

About This Course

This course teaches you HTML5 from the very beginning.

You will learn to build real webpages step by step.

No prior experience needed.
Just bring your curiosity!

```
commonMisconceptions: Never use heading tags just to make text bigger — use them only for actual headings and structure.
Use strong for importance and b only for stylistic bold; they look the same but mean different things to screen readers.
Multiple spaces in HTML collapse into one — use CSS for spacing, not blank spaces or extra br tags.
subtopics.realWorldAnalogy: Headings in HTML are like chapter titles and section headers in a textbook. They help readers (and search engines) quickly scan the structure and find what they need.

subtopics.title: HTML Comments & Best Practices
subtopics.content:
As your HTML files grow larger, comments help you and other developers understand what each section does. Comments are ignored by the browser — they never appear on the page. **Writing a comment:** ```html

This paragraph IS visible on the page.

``` **Multi-line comments:** ```html
...
``` **Commenting out code temporarily** (useful for debugging): ```html

Only this paragraph shows.

``` **Best practices to follow from day one:** - Always include `` at the top. - Always close your tags — an unclosed tag can break the entire layout below it. - Use **indentation** (2 or 4 spaces) to show nesting clearly. - Use **lowercase** for all tag names — while HTML is not case-sensitive, lowercase is the professional standard. - Always add a `lang` attribute to your html tag for accessibility. - Write **meaningful** titles in your title tag — not just 'Page' or 'Untitled'. Here is an example of well-structured, clean HTML: ```html CodeBay – Lesson 1

Introduction to HTML5

What You Will Learn

In this lesson, you will discover the building blocks of every webpage on the internet.

``` Clean, readable HTML is a habit worth building from your very first file.
commonMisconceptions: Comments are NOT secure — anyone can see them by viewing your page source. Never put passwords or sensitive info in comments.
Indentation does not affect how the page looks — it only helps humans read the code.
subtopics.realWorldAnalogy: HTML comments are like sticky notes inside a folder of documents. Only the person reading the folder sees them — the audience of the final presentation never does.

recommendedTools.name: Visual Studio Code
recommendedTools.reason: Best free code editor for beginners and professionals

recommendedTools.name: Chrome Developer Tools
recommendedTools.reason: Built into Chrome – inspect any webpage's HTML

furtherReading: MDN Web Docs: HTML Basics (free, authoritative)
HTML Living Standard (official specification – very advanced)

glossary.definition:
A keyword enclosed in angle brackets (like

) that tells the browser how to display content.

The combination of an opening tag, content, and a closing tag (e.g.,

Hello

is a paragraph element).
Extra information added to an opening tag (like href in <a href='url'>). We'll learn attributes in Lesson 2.
Using tags that describe their meaning (like <strong> for importance) instead of just visual appearance.
A declaration at the top of an HTML file that tells the browser which version of HTML is being used.
Information about the webpage stored in the head section, such as the character set, viewport settings, and page title.
